text.title: User Interview Rules and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Neutrality principles, facilitation rules, synthesis quality requirements, and agent limitations.

rule.statement: Listen more than talk; the interviewer's words should be fewer than 20% of the session transcript.
rule.rationale: Talking more than the participant signals that the interviewer is not genuinely curious, closing down disclosure and producing thin data.

rule.statement: Do not react positively or negatively to answers; stay neutral throughout.
rule.rationale: Positive reactions (nodding, "great!") bias subsequent answers toward what the interviewer appeared to reward; neutrality preserves honest disclosure.

rule.statement: Record sessions (with consent) even when taking notes.
rule.rationale: Notes miss quote-level detail; transcripts enable the evidence-backed synthesis that makes findings credible to stakeholders.

rule.statement: Always provide verbatim source material when asking the agent to "find examples" or extract quotes.
rule.rationale: Agent will hallucinate plausible-sounding quotes if not given actual transcript text; synthesis must be grounded in provided source.

rule.statement: Insight statements synthesized by agent must be validated against source notes by the researcher before sharing with stakeholders.
rule.rationale: Agent may introduce framing bias when rewriting observations into insights; researcher validation is the quality gate.

rule.statement: Sanitize transcripts (replace participant names with IDs) before passing to external LLM APIs.
rule.rationale: Interview transcripts often contain PII; participant privacy must be protected under applicable regulations.

antipattern.description: Poor recruitment — interviewing participants who do not match the target user profile.
antipattern.reason: Insights from the wrong users produce design decisions that serve a population that does not exist or will not use the product.

antipattern.description: Not probing — accepting surface-level answers without follow-up.
antipattern.reason: First answers are often rehearsed or socially acceptable; the real insight lives in the follow-up ("why is that?", "can you give me an example?").

antipattern.description: No analysis — collecting data without synthesizing it into insights and sharing with the team.
antipattern.reason: Unanalyzed interview data has zero design impact; synthesis is not optional.
